title: How the WA man charged with plotting a terrorist act legally kept his guns
author: Keane Bourke
fullText: More than 4,000 firearms have so far been seized by Western Australian authorities as part of a crackdown limiting who is allowed to have them.According to police, they came from outlaw motorcycle gang members, family and domestic violence perpetrators and people convicted of serious offences.It was part of the state's transition to tougher firearms laws with fewer reasons for people to have a licence, mental health checks and — for the first time in Australia — limits on the number of firearms someone can own.But 20-year-old Jayson Joseph Michaels and his father were allowed to keep their substantial collection even after transitioning to the new laws.Mr Michaels has now become the first person in WA to be charged with preparing for a terrorist act, and police have now seized seven guns from the family’s Bindoon home.The reason they had not acted sooner highlights the limitations of the state’s gun laws in ensuring public safety.Tougher restrictionsUnder the new laws, WA's police commissioner has wide-ranging discretion to decide who can have firearms.As soon as the commissioner believes it is not "in the interests of public safety" for someone to have a gun licence, they must cancel it.Police have expanded their power to decide who can have firearms under WA's new gun laws.  (ABC News: Keane Bourke)Licences are also restricted to those deemed a "fit and proper person".There's no definition of what that means, but the commissioner can consider everything from someone's "conduct and behaviour" and their "views, opinions and attitudes" to their "living or domestic circumstances".And it includes the licence holder’s close associates, like relatives, housemates and business partners.Firearms taken from sovereign citizensIt is these powers which last year allowed police to cancel or suspend the licences of 44 people they said were sovereign citizens – meaning they believe the government is illegitimate.It was intelligence that allowed WA police to take action in that operation.More than 4,000 firearms have been seized in WA under a crackdown on gun ownership. (ABC News: Berge Breiland)Without intelligence, there's no action.And the message from authorities is clear: intelligence of radicalisation is getting harder to come by."We're seeing that radicalisation occurs primarily through often digital means, through engagements on forums, channels in particular messaging apps," Curtin Extremism Research Network director Ben Rich said this week."Not through the kind of traditional organising in groups that we sort of got used to through post-911 and terrorism during the Cold War."Ben Rich says radicalisation occurs primarily online.  (ABC News: Cason Ho )Breaking into a big group can quickly provide police with information about a large number of people.But when individuals are being radicalised online by watching content, or participating in smaller groups, that work becomes much harder."It doesn't take years, sometimes it doesn't even take a year, [it takes] a matter of months where people can have their thoughts escalated," Premier Roger Cook said yesterday.What do authorities sayWA Police Commissioner Col Blanch's message has been simple."On almost all occasions, it's actually the members of our community that come forward and help us to investigate these matters," he said on Friday after announcing the terror charge had been laid.The guns found at Jayson Joseph Michaels's family home were owned by him and his father. (Supplied: WA Police)Premier Roger Cook stressed that point, saying the solution "may not be a police response, but the solution for them is always to connect and to understand".Part of that solution could also be taking someone’s firearms away, at least temporarily.Col Blanch says the community play a big role in helping the terror investigation.  (ABC News: Andrew O'Connor)Dr Rich believes the problem runs deeper than that. "It's about taking a look at the foundations of our society, things like the social safety net … housing, healthcare, professional futures," he said."When I particularly talk to young men, there's often this sense of despair that there's nothing for them to do and that maybe some radical change has to occur."So we have to be really thinking about how do we strengthen that social contract that our forebears got, you know, the fair go that we like to talk about in society."Enforcement solutions can make a difference, and no doubt police will be considering whether there was more they could have done to intervene in this case earlier.But the real solution to improving safety is out of their hands.It rests with politicians, community leaders, and ultimately everyone in the community working to reduce division and bring our fractured society back together.
summary: Western Australia is transitioning to tougher firearms laws — but despite the changes, 20-year-old Jayson Joseph Michaels and his father were allowed to keep their collection of seven guns at the family's Bindoon home.
